content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 6 – “P” for ProductPart 2 - Product and service decisions
Chapter 7 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part  2 - Product and service decisions

Product and service decisions (1 of 7)
Marketers make product and service decisions at three levels:
individual product and service decisions
product line decisions
product mix decision.

Product and service decisions (2 of 7)
Individual product and service decisions
Figure 7.2 Individual product decisions

Product and service decisions (3 of 7)
Individual product and service decisions (cont.)
Product and service attributes
Product quality refers to the characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied customer needs.
Product quality has two dimensions:
level, i.e. performance quality: the ability of a product to perform its functions
consistency, i.e. conformance quality: freedom from defects and consistency in delivering a targeted level of performance.

Product and service decisions (4 of 7)
Individual product and service decisions (cont.)
Other types of product and service decisions include:
branding
packaging – the activities of designing and producing the container or wrapper for a product
labelling
product support services.
Q: Give some examples of product support services. Why are these important?

Product and service decisions (5 of 7)
Product line decisions
A product line is a group of products that are closely related because they function in a similar manner, are sold to the same customer groups, are marketed through the same types of outlets or fall within given price ranges.
The main product line decision is product line depth, i.e. the number of products in the line.
A company can expand its product line by:
product line filling
product line stretching.

Figure 7.3
Product line stretching decisions
Q: When would a company use product line stretching? When would it use product line filling?

Product and service decisions (7 of 7)
Product mix decisions
A product mix is the set of all product lines and items that a particular seller offers for sale.
Product mix decisions form the basis of a company’s product strategy. They include:
width – the number of different product lines
length – the total number of items the company carries
depth – the number of versions of each product in the line
consistency – how closely related the various product lines are.
Q: How could a company use these four product mix dimensions to increase its business?

Product and service decisions
A: Product Help Line: often customers do not get the results they expect from the product. If they call the Help Line the people there can advise them on how to use the product to get the best results. This helps to ensure that the customer expectations are met > Satisfaction > and hopefully they will become a loyal customer.
A: Stretching so that it can offer products to other market segments, typically lower-priced and/or higher-priced, e.g. BMW stretched their 3-series line downwards to the 1-series and upwards to the 5-series.
Line Filling: to offer more choices to current and competitors’ customers within the same market segment. Hopefully this will increase sales.
Q: Give some examples of product support services. Why are these important?
Q: When would a company use product line stretching? When would it use product line filling?

Product and service decisions
How could a company use these four product mix dimensions to increase its business?
A: A product line is a group of products that are closely related because they function in a similar manner, are sold to the same customer groups, are marketed through the same types of outlets or fall within given price ranges.
Therefore, increasing width, that is adding lines, means that the company can offer products to new customer groups, sell in a new set of retail outlets, and /or place new products in different price ranges.

Product and service decisions
A: length – the total number of items the company carries. Therefore, increasing length = adding more items to the range offered. This would give current customers more choice – so that if they are variety-seeking they can get their variety within the range of items the company offers rather than getting variety by buying a competitor’s item/s. It would also appeal to competitors’ customers who had previously not bought from us because the particular type of item they wanted was not offered by us.
How could a company use these four product mix dimensions to increase its business?

Product and service decisions
A: depth – the number of versions of each product in the line. Offering more versions would offer more variety as discussed earlier,  appeal to competitors’ customers who want that version, might appeal to a new customer groups (e.g. consumers vs. professionals, cold-climate vs hot-climate etc.)
Q: How could a company use these four product mix dimensions to increase its business?
A: consistency – how closely related the various product lines are. Increasing consistency, that is, more-closely relating the lines, would mean that the company’s product range will cover more of the market = fewer gaps in the line and therefore fewer places where competitors can position their products in parts of the market where there are no or few competitors. For example, Toyota have very few gaps in their line.
